conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let me first review the official eligibility rules, and then I'll ask you some questions.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility. Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children for whom you'd like to claim Child Benefit? If yes, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have one child I'd like to ask about - my son Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Alex is 17, I need to check if he meets the requirements for Child Benefit to continue beyond age 16.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in an approved training program)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in approved education. He's started an apprenticeship actually.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to clarify one important detail:

**Is Alex's apprenticeship in England specifically, or is it in Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I now need to ask a few more questions about your living situation and responsibility for Alex.

**Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester actually - and Alex lives here at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the information you've provided, I need to deliver some unfortunate news regarding your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Assessment Complete**

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: While you meet the basic requirements (living in the UK with Alex and being responsible for him), the rules specifically state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England. Even though Alex is only 17 and Child Benefit can normally continue until age 20 for young people in approved education or training, apprenticeships in England are specifically excluded from this provision.

If you were already receiving Child Benefit for Alex, the payments would have stopped when he started his apprenticeship. If you have any questions or need further clarification, you can contact the Child Benefit Office directly.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, apprenticeships in England are specifically excluded from this provision. Since Alex lives with you in the UK and no one else is claiming for him, you would normally meet the basic eligibility criteria, but the fact that he has started an apprenticeship in England means Child Benefit will stop.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old son Alex. While they meet the basic residency and responsibility requirements, Alex has started an apprenticeship in England, which is a specific disqualifying circumstance under Child Benefit rules. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, apprenticeships in England are specifically excluded from this provision. Since Alex lives with you in the UK and no one else is claiming for him, you would normally meet the basic eligibility criteria, but the fact that he has started an apprenticeship in England means Child Benefit will stop.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old son Alex. While they meet the basic residency and responsibility requirements, Alex has started an apprenticeship in England, which is a specific disqualifying circumstance under Child Benefit rules. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
